title: Better Beds
text:
I don’t remember my first bed. I’m betting it was just a standard spring and frame setup. The first bed I remember was my water bed. It was so high up off the ground. Under it six dresser drawers resided unused. The headboard area was what I loved; it had sliding door cabinets and tons of room for my Legos to stay. I think that’s when I started staying in bed for activities. Right now at this moment I am in my bed.

I read books here, I watch the fan blades spin here, and I hear new songs here. This is my area for anything and everything. I would go to ‘bed’ when my parents told me to, but I would pop in a cassette and spend the next few hours listening and building Legos. Having imaginary space fights and living in my own world, till my eyes got too tired and I had to succumb to sleep. I liked that bed, but I found lots of faults with it. Waterbeds are not my cup of tea.

My next bed that I remember was the California King. It was huge. You could fit four average sized people in it with no issue. During the life span of this bed I was becoming more social and it was often the gathering place of my friends. We would huddle around my laptop on it watching funny videos or spend hours just talking about everything and nothing. It once again set upon me this idea that beds are not just for sleeping.

So now in my life I am in a state of flux. My life has no true stability. Reflected in that is my current bed. It is ten inches of memory foam. Don’t misunderstand me, this is probably the most comfortable sleeping situation I’ve ever had. It’s that I can move it in ten minutes on my own that shows my mindset. With this bed it’s all about that safety and comfort. That regardless of what happens to me, I can roll up my bed and move it with me.

I wonder about what my next bed will be and what stage of life it will show me I am in. I hope it ties together all my favorite parts of my previous beds. That whatever dreaming I do in it, whoever comes over and watches funny videos, or however stable I am. That it’s a happy place full of comfort and pillows. Lots of pillows.

title: Colorado Combine
text:
I have this recurring image in my head. It’s a pan through a car, with beautiful landscapes passing by quickly. The shot goes to the back seat where a boy is looking out the window daydreaming. It sits on this shot for a while with Joanna Newsom playing. Then it snaps to black. That will be the start of my movie if I ever make one. I know it’s been done, but it just speaks to me. I like to feel how the boy feels, curious and calm. He is aware that where he is being taken is worth it, that he must just wait.

My adventure begins tomorrow, to Braden and Rachel’s wedding! The story around them fascinates me. Two people who for years were basically family. Two people no one could have ever guessed getting together. Yet with time and growing, they both became compatible people. They happened upon each other and in that moment saw something different than the thousands of other times they were around each other.

I enjoy being around them. They radiate happiness. This is partly because of the relationship, but mainly because they are both genuinely happy people. I have seen them both in a variety of moods and states. But the default for them is overwhelming joy. That might be something that drew them closer.

I know Rachel as a little sister. In the early times she was just around. I don’t know if she was just shy or if we couldn’t hear her. She soon became a young woman full of ideas and hopes. She was constantly up for an adventure with Kevin, Seth, and I. Most of my thoughts of her are tied up in her kindness. She just has this ability to be with people and put them at ease. She rarely is thinking of herself, constantly pushing outwards trying to see what she can do for others.

Braden at his core shares these qualities. At times I felt out of place, as if I didn’t fit in the group of older boys. He would change that instantly with one of his hugs. He infuses you with love and you understand him. He wants that back; he wants you to accept and love him back. I remember the day my misconceptions were broken. I thought of him as a jock type, not an intellectual person. Then he started talking to me about books and all the fiction that he loved. I was just a dumb kid who thought all people were one thing. I couldn’t have been more wrong.

So off we go to a wedding. It’s in a beautiful area with my favorite people. I am expectant with the memories I will make. With the stories that will be told. The conversations of dreams and love that will happen. I expect much, because my family rarely disappoints. So here is to Braden and Rachel! Never think it can’t happen. Never underestimate the person you can become. Be filled with joy and happiness. It’s time for a wedding!

title: Created Cabin
text:
The crisp cold air slightly burns as you breathe it deeply. Glints of sunshine are appearing through the trees. Fog wanders close to the ground. It sounds almost silent. A few bird calls here and there. Wind rustling the leaves. The air has an energy about it. Daybreak is here. Time is now. Nothing is going to distract you or stop your plans for the day. This is the forest, your forest.

I have spent countless hours daydreaming about this scene. Everyone has a perfect place. A moment that can only exist in their heads. Mine takes place in this forest. I have spent years here. Learning and building everything I need. Over time my hands have become more calloused and my mind has become made over. Changed from the present constant need for attention. I am an older, calmer man, at ease.

It’s this cabin, this idea that I cling to when life seems bleak. It’s a log cabin built in a simple manner. One front door, a few windows that stay open most of the year. A stone chimney on the side with white smoke billowing out. Every log in the house I picked and cut down myself. This house wasn’t built quickly. It took months of hard work and preparation. There is a cellar on the side dug down below the cabin.

Around the side is a modest garden with a few vegetables growing along with a few chosen flowers. The chickens have a coop they rest in at night, but they are free to wander during the day. If you walk to the south for a moment or two you come across a fast moving brook. With water so clean and pure you can simply cup your hand and enjoy. There is a simple hammock not far away. It is placed in an area where at two in the afternoon the sun shines on it, warming the body for a perfect nap.

Of course my loyal dog is with me most of the day. She wanders within a fifty foot radius of me. Looking for things to bring to my attention or for sticks that I can toss. Protection is not an issue; this forest is a peaceful place. Over time I have learned how to walk on the forest floor making little to no sound. Learning the best ways around, becoming friends with the trees.

Inside the cabin is just what you would expect. It is just one large room. A stove and sink on one side, a couch and rocking chairs near the front windows. The bed in the back corner. The bed would be large with a multitude of blankets and pillows. Simple quilts would be laying over the chairs in the room. Books everywhere, most of them about how to build and garden. Handmade journals with my explorations written in them line a shelf on the wall.

A good friend asked me one night what I wanted. I replied, “Some toast with jam and some tea.” We talked about those two simple things till sleep captured us both. This toast with homemade apricot jam and fresh churned butter. That hot cup of creamy, slightly bitter tea. They both exist in this cabin. That is the delightful gift that is offered in this cabin when you enter. Another friend told me that she hopes I find my cabin one day.

She knew that this daydream, this vivid world in my head, was what I lived for. That it was a goal I must attain one day. Even now, years later, I still daydream about the same place. I ponder the endless activities that I want to accomplish there. I know that yearning causes reality to be less than it should be. That this place will never be quite what it is in my head. I still choose to dream and hope. Hope that I can make this a reality one day. That I can learn to be a person that has all these things. “Love hopes all things”

~Tom

title: Dear Drama
text:
It all started three months ago. With a missed call at seven in the morning. Would I like to be in the drama? Of course. I would have to see if I could get off work for it though. One simple call to my boss and it was all worked out. My next thirteen weekends were booked up. My social life would have to be put on hold. Turns out that most of my friends were in the drama too. This was my first one though; I was in untested waters.

I had trust in my directors though. The nerves slowly drifted away as we listened to the drama once through as a group. Once everyone got their parts and heard the lines. Some quick cast changes happened, but no one was flustered. Everything went smoothly. The next weekend we started. Getting our positioning down. Feeling out the timings. When to start heading on to the stage. For me it was a lot of down time too.

I have a small part in the drama. It might be like 30 seconds of stage time. So much of my practices were spent in the back talking to others. Or reading new Watchtowers. I even wrote a few blogs there. I am astounded how everything came together though. The hours of labor that went in to the drama. From the costumes, wigs, and beards. To the props that had to be made. Around a hundred people toiled and sacrificed for this drama. I heard a few people say that they were tired. No complaints though. There was excitement when we got a day off though.

When you see the final product though. When it all comes together, it feels seamless or simple. Things that these people spent countless hours on blur into a one hour performance. I will never take another drama for granted. It is hard work. There is a payoff though. I think during every single practice I would get panicky and nervous. During the real thing though? Calm as a cow at pasture. It was definitely holy spirit working through me. As soon as it started, it was over.

I’m happy that we get to do it once more. That I get to see my drama family working as a large well oiled machine again. I’m hoping that feeling of calmness sets in and everything goes according to plan. The only part I’m not looking forward to is the makeup. Ladies, why do you do this to yourself?! My skin hates it. I couldn’t be happier that I gave up those weekends. That Danny and Tracy thought of me. That I get to be a small part of a big thing. It was a true honor, something I will never forget.

title: Fancy day
text:
It just started as a simple conversation near the end of last year. Friday night dinners had been going great and we wanted more of that. We wanted to have something to dress up for and think about for weeks to come. So we talked and talked. Most of the ideas Seth and I have die there. This one planted in both of our minds and grew into a lovely day. We had a fancy day.

When I was younger, I didn’t appreciate things the way I have learned to. My brother Kevin would take me to great places. I can think of the many times he took me to museums around DFW. I fought him for a while; I didn’t think it was worth spending the time on. But it only took a few visits to the DMA, the Nasher sculpture museum, and The Modern and I was hooked. That was Kevin’s way. He wouldn’t eat food that wasn’t up to his standards. Wouldn’t shop at places that didn’t meet his whims, and he loved showing people the better things they could be doing. He was right in doing all these things.

Why do anything that doesn’t meet your expectations or exceed them? He valued his time and money spent. We all would mock him, but after some time these qualities passed on to us as well. Not in the fullest Kevinisms, but so much as I get upset when someone wants to eat at a Chili’s or Applebee’s. I refuse to go to places that are cash only and will now return, call, or write about items that don’t meet expectations. This is where the root of fancy day began.

Since Kevin had left Texas for bigger waters there was a struggle to fill the gap that he left. For a tastemaker to emerge and make choices. I know for myself it took time to come to my personal solution. Be my own tastemaker. Find places I get excited about and go to them. Friday night dinners began because I missed seeing people on a routine basis. The Fort Worth magazine put out an issue that covered the thirty best meals for under ten dollars. It gave us places to go and experiences to have. It was a match that lit the flame of adventure.

So how was fancy day? It began around one with a quick bite at Salsa Fuego. A Mexican restaurant that from the outside looks far from fancy, but looks are deceptive. I had the chorizo burger — yes, that exists. A thick patty of Mexican sausage with a lot of fixings, melted cheese, and a fried egg on top. It is all the best parts of breakfast in burger form. If you go to have this you will have to ask for it, as it is not on the menu and they rarely have it in stock. Anything you order, though, will be delicious.

Then we wound up at the Modern. Sadly the Kimbell was closed to members only that day. I love the Modern, its big bleak stone walls and cold feeling. I had been a few times in the last few months, so I had seen most everything. Nonetheless I love seeing people look at art. Trying to grasp at feelings that are often buried. Trying to make sense of silly shapes and blurry photos. Wondering if everyone is feeling or seeing, understanding anything.

Seeing as we had some time to do away with, we went to the trendiest street in Fort Worth: Magnolia! All up and down the street are some of the best bars and restaurants in Fort Worth. One of the newest is a coffee bar/brew pub called Brewed. It has a great vibe to it. I describe it to people as being the modern version of the coffee shop in the TV show Friends. Had some great beers and coffee drinks. Watched the sun slowly sink into the horizon and talked about life. Then we made our move to cheese!

Magnolia Cheese Co is one of the newest places on the street. If you love wine and cheese, this is the place to be. I have yet to try any of their many sandwich choices. We tried one of their cheese platters. For seventeen dollars you get a small sampling of five different cheeses along with garnishes and some bread to wash it all down with. It’s not something you go get every day, but on a fancy day or a great night on the town it is a great stop.

Then we headed to Bass Hall to see the Fort Worth symphony and Nobu the pianist. It is a grand building that matched our outfits for the first time that whole day. It truly was a fancy place. The first thing I noticed was the age disparity. We were the youngest people there by a large margin. But everyone I ran into and chatted with was kind and helpful. The seats were in the nosebleeds, but we are a bunch of poor kids so it felt right. I wish I would have had glasses for it though. At that distance all I could see was odd blurs of people and instruments.

The concert was astounding, to say the least. To see so many people in such harmony and time was a miracle. The sounds that reached my ears felt like waves of emotions in the sea. Ebbing and flowing with the old pieces they were playing. Then they brought out Nobu, a blind Asian pianist. As my cousin Seth said after, “His fingers were like butter.” Not only was he a genius, but he played the whole twenty-five minutes from memory. Although for him I bet it was less memory and more like he felt the music. The part that confused me was how he got clapped for, walked off, came back, and got clapped for again — and this happened at least five times. While his talent was amazing, I felt like the first two standing ovations were plenty.

After that it was late and we retreated to Cafe Brazil, a go-to for after-show food. After parking and walking to the Cafe we passed a rowdy bar filled with college students. I watched them as they went along their night, talking loudly and enjoying their Saturday. Everyone, it seems, is looking for their night. Their night to get with friends and talk about the past and look for new adventures. I find with time that most great adventures have to be planned slightly and left with room for excitement. I also felt bad for the kids in that bar. That they would never know the day that I just had. The wonderful time I had in a suit with five other friends. I recommend you plan a day to just be fancy with some friends.

Originally published at

amuncie.eatprescott.com

.

title: Fatal Flaws.
text:
I’m in a good head space, so it must be a good time to write about my flaws. It’s been something I have wanted to type out for a while. Not all of them. Just a few. I think that everyone has fatal flaws. Inclinations that if left unchecked or given fuel could lead to death. Sometimes the death is real, like in the case of addicts. Sometimes the death is not physical but mental.

A person can have only one, sometimes many. I have a few. It takes time to see them for what they are. They make you who you are. I don’t think it is possible to hate them. It makes you who you are. I want to write about my depression and anxiety. I do want to note right now that I have not been diagnosed, nor am I offering any medical advice. I’m just sharing what I live through.

My childhood was a happy one. My parents provided everything for me. When I hit my teenage years my emotions flared. Doing what they were supposed to do at that age. But the overage of sadness never went away. People rarely talk about depression, and a lot of people still think it’s something you can just snap out of. So for a long part of my life I just viewed it as something everyone went through.

The anxiety was present at the same time. It can build and grow depending on how much fuel I feed it. I worry, a lot. I view that as a stage before anxiousness. When it consumes me, it causes panic attacks. That is when it cripples me. Many times I’ve been in a group of people and I just disappear to find somewhere to be alone. To calm myself down. It’s not something I let people know about me generally. To have some semblance of normalcy, I guess.

Both feelings are overwhelming. Often seemingly impossible to overcome. Depression feels like you are in a world without color. You can’t call people or text anyone. The idea of doing things makes you feel sick. Sleeping and mind numbing activities are all you want. Most of the time reading was my escape. To forget everything, jump into a fake world, and let my imagination run free.

Anxiety feels like you are in a room with walls that keep getting closer every time you blink. Closer and closer till you can’t move. You keep your eyes open so they won’t move in, but they do. Pressing on your skin. Tighter and tighter. Trapped. Overwhelmed. Big empty spaces and music help me with this. After a while I found that reading poetry helped relax me as well.

Without a doubt my beliefs and faith have saved me. An understanding that this isn’t normal. That I can be fixed. That no one will have to live with it. It lets me keep focus. Keep moving. Stay happy for the most part.

How fatal are these flaws though? Without my coping systems and the truth, I believe they would destroy me. Oftentimes it has hurt my life. The amount of hours that I have hid in my room, slept away sadness, is uncountable. They would have consumed me. I would have not died physically, but mentally.

So I watch them with an intense gaze. I have dear friends that watch as well. We keep each other away from these things. I know for a fact that the things I go through are mild compared to a vast majority of people. I hope no matter your fatal flaw, you find it. Learn it. Find a way to overcome or cope with it. Confide in close loved ones. Keep moving forward. Whatever it is, it can be overcome. Tomorrow is another day. Positivity is your best friend. I believe in you. You can make it. You can make it.

tom.

title: Fossil Creek Part — 1
text:
I just couldn’t understand why he wasn’t responding. My only solution was to go back to sleep and try again later. It must have been 6AM when I started waking up. It wasn’t till seven that I stood up and set in my mind that I was staying awake. When I asked him this time he responded to all my questions. It turns out that before, what I thought was an elegantly worded question manifested itself as incoherent mumbling to someone still asleep. Still hours before we needed to leave, I ate some leftover pizza and watched YouTube videos.

In his small room, perfectly designed for one person, we had crammed five. It was a flop house right now — a place to sleep and nothing more. That was all the room you had. The other three started to wake. Stretches and grumblings were heard. Everyone started to dress and prepare for the day. On this Thursday the plan was to drive out to an abandoned power plant, then hike one mile to a natural hot spring. That was the plan.

After planning what to dress in and what to bring, we set off in the car toward Jerome. My dress was simple: shorts and shirt. I made the choice to bring my backpack with me. That seemed like a good idea at the time. We stopped to get fuel soon after leaving. The gas stations here have post offices in them and they sell just about anything you could ever want. I saw a roll of toilet paper and bought it. You never know what you will need in the middle of nowhere.

To get to Jerome you must go up a mountain road. The town is on the other side of the mountain, exactly where you wouldn’t expect it. It’s a town built on the side of the mountain. It was a copper mine at one time; the town grew organically around it. To this day it seems like a run-down place, existing only because of its location. We ate at a nice place that exists only because of its location. It must be odd to exist only because of your location.

After eating I wanted to go look in a store where they sell interesting rocks. As you walk in there’s a sign proclaiming: “No Photos.” The more you look around, the more matching signs you will find. If you know anything about Asa, it’s that he’s a compulsive recorder of memories. I have a theory that it’s part of his hoarding tendencies, but I love having lots of photos of memories, so it is a welcome compulsion. I waited with my back turned expecting to hear “Please don’t take any photos.” What I heard was different.

A quick shuffling of a middle-aged, overweight woman and the shout “Can’t you read the signs? This is a private place! I don’t go into your home and take photos!” She was visibly insane. If you ever find yourself in Jerome, do me a favor and don’t give

AzDiscoveries

any business. Kevin, of course, rebuked her for the way she reacted and we swiftly left. After leaving that town we only stopped once more for some last-minute supplies.

Once we got to the dirt road, I knew it would only be twenty miles in. On a paved road it would have taken twenty-five minutes tops. On this unmaintained, unpaved road it took close to an hour. An hour of the bumpiest, rockiest driving I’ve ever been subjected to. It felt like miniature mules were kicking you over and over for an hour.

Quickly I knew that the whole gang wanted to turn back. No one wants to be that person; we gritted our teeth and headed forward. With every passing second my brain was telling me how every inch forward was an inch we would have to come back. I suppressed those thoughts as best I could. The view was incredible. Raw, untouched, jagged land, marred only by randomly placed power lines and drainage pipes. A palpable feeling took hold of us; we knew we were going somewhere not many had gone.

Over the day we met at least thirty different people. Whether passing them on the road or hiking alongside them. It was more than I had expected to see this far into the wilderness. The last few feet of the road were the worst, but we made it. Asa parked the car and everyone loaded up their gear and started down the trail.

The book that told us about this hike said it was an easy one.

title: Fossil Creek Part — 2
text:
So I thought it would be fine. I could load up all that I needed and go forward. My pack is heavy, close to impossible for a normal person. When we took off it felt full, but nothing that I couldn’t handle. Quickly the boys got distracted and searched an abandoned power plant. I took the time to sit down and look at rocks. It was during this time that I found the only rock I brought back to Texas. It is a creamy gray stone with stripes of red. I plan on smoothing it down and making a necklace out of it soon.

Once the boys returned we hit the trail. It followed the river close to the shore. Quickly it got muddy and close to the hill next to us. The river, it turns out, was running high and fast that day; to us though that was normal. I knew something was wrong when the trail stopped. To the left was the river moving fast, to the right a steep cliff face. Seth quickly scrambled up the side of it and deemed it the path we must take.

I wasn’t going to be able to handle this. I was out of breath and tired at that point. I turned around to look back; there were two men and a five or six year old girl coming up fast behind us. The man leading them looked to be a natural at this. His life was most likely filled with hiking and adventuring. This was when he informed us that the river was high and up this way was the only choice. Up the cliff, then up a small hill to an old road, then it was a quick walk to where the river crossing is.

I set my mind to it. No couldn’t be a choice. I wasn’t going to make the whole group turn back because of my life choices. I wasn’t going to let a six year old do things I couldn’t. Kevin guided my every step. It was slow going and quite terrifying the whole way up, but I made it. I could feel my heart pounding out of my chest. Dehydration was setting in. I still had the hill to climb to get to the road. Seth stayed with me. Helped me find my way. It was at the top that we found an old cattle pen.

Seth had been carrying my backpack since the cliff. It was heavy, much too heavy for this hike. I chose to leave it at the cattle pen. There are no thieves in the forest / desert, wherever we were. After sitting in the pen for a while my vigor had been restored. It was an old road that sloped down. The orange marker where we needed to cross the river was close. So we set off; with only a gallon of water to carry I felt as light as a bird.

As we approached the river we caught sight of the two men and little girl again. We went to where they were and asked a few more questions. This was where we would cross. The river at this point was up to my hips and moving fast — nothing I couldn’t handle though. Seth went to find me a big walking stick so I could have good footing on the crossing. While he and Kevin went to get one, I watched and listened. The men were arguing. The one with all the cameras and bags was fighting the crossing.

He wanted to take his things, the river was too fast, and how would his dog make it? The doubt had rooted itself in his heart. I knew he would not be going across. The other man, the adventurer, pleaded with him. Offered many solutions. They came back with my stick. To another person it would be a large branch, but for me it was perfection.

The water was cold, ice cold. On this hot day though I didn’t mind. Crossing the river was a delight. I love every moment I get to be in water. We reached the middle island and crossed over. What we saw was dejecting. Fast moving deep water, up to your shoulders deep. The man left his kid near us and tried different paths. Seth got some rope out and did the same. I looked up the river and saw rapids.

It wasn’t happening.

We could see the hot springs. Right across the river. 200 feet away. All that hiking and hard work. In my mind I wanted to feel dejected. I couldn’t allow myself; we had a hike back to the car and a long drive on the terrible road after that. I had to stay positive and happy or my mood would have affected everyone. We came to the choice to go back. The sun was going down in an hour or so. It was time to pull the plug.

The hike back was swift. With the road and not having that cliff to go down, it took half the time. Hot sweaty boys smelling of river water and sadness filled the car. The road was unforgiving. Kevin had a place for us to go though. A river we could swim in. After about twenty minutes, we made it. It was beautiful.

A clear river with a small waterfall upstream and the sun setting downstream. On the other shore was a tall, long dead tree that had a rope hanging from it. The water here was colder than the other water. It was close to unbearable. The kind of water you have to toss yourself in and wait for numbness to set in. Seth and Moses started swinging on the rope into the water. Kevin and I jumped in from the other side. Asa recorded our jumps and we just let the whole day wash away.

Although the objective was a wash, we still had a great day. I did things that I thought were impossibilities for me. Friends — no, brothers — will push you. Make you try. It might seem frustrating, feel like they don’t know your limitations. I think they know better than I do though. It’s a trust I have built up with them. That they will never put me into a situation that I can’t handle. I loved that day. I now expect more out of myself. One day we will go back. We will make it to the hot springs.

title: Frank the Fox
text:
Frank was a fox. Today he was looking for a new place to make his den. With swift, deliberate movements he bounded from rock to branch. Keeping his eyes open to movement, ears alert to strange sounds. In his mouth he had a rock. It was a shiny rock that sparked when in sunlight. It was to be put in the new den, to let others know that he was special.

He had found it when he was a pup. It was on the side of a river. His mother had told him it was foolish; foxes had no possessions. She told him he could keep the rock if he left the den. So he did. Out on his own, skittering around being free. The first few nights out alone were hard. But Frank was a smart fox.

He found an outcropping of rocks near the lake. One of the nooks would make a perfect area. He used his paws to dig in slightly. Then pounced around outside at the grass. Biting at tufts of grass, bringing it into the new den for bedding. At the back of the new den Frank put his rock, his possession.

If Frank was ever to bring a girl fox back to his den, she would surely like it. On this night though he would sleep alone. He rarely dreamed of anything other than chasing a big wolf away from a kill. He would awake proud of this figmental success. Today he would look around at the new area. Find places to hunt.

After exploring the inward forest for most of the day he turned back to the lake. A frog sat on the shore. A nice meal, thought Frank. Crouching as low as he could get, he slowly and methodically inched closer and closer. After agonizing moments of movement, he was seconds away from pouncing. His muscles ached from being tensed for so long. Frank urged for the taste of this frog. For the caloric energy it would give him. For surviving another day.

A breath away from pouncing, a fish surfaced. “HEY, hey, what are you doing?” the fish asked. Frank was distracted for a moment. The frog leapt away. Missed opportunity. Upset, he shouted at the fish, “I was hunting, but you have ruined that.” The fish looked with one eye for a while. “Do you wish to have the knowledge of the fish?”

Frank knew two things. Fish only had memories that lasted ten minutes tops, and fish knew nothing. Incensed, he said with false sincerity, “What could the wise fish teach me?” bowing slightly as he spoke. “How to swim as a fish does,” the fish said plainly.

The conversation went on. Frank kept asking questions and the fish gave replies. According to the fish, the reason foxes couldn’t swim like the fish was because they foolishly kept their mouths closed while underwater. Also he needed to wag his butt back and forth to move around. To Frank everything the fish said made sense.

The next day they talked more. Frank ignored his hunger. He wanted to be the first fox to learn this new ability. The fish told him to come out to the middle of the lake to try swimming like a fish. He padded out into the water. Kicked hard and fast to stay above the water and followed the fish.

Once out to the middle the fish said, “Just do everything I taught you.” Still looking at him with that one unblinking eye. His legs were tired from treading water. It was time to become the first fox to swim like a fish. He took his last kick. Pushed all the air out of his lungs. Sank down. Waited. Opened his mouth, took in huge gulps of water while wagging his butt and tail for movement.

The sharp pangs of fear kicked in. Nothing felt right. Frank kept trying to breathe in. Tried kicking his paws. He was so tired. Why wasn’t this working? Frank’s last thought was “The fish. He told me it would work.”

title: Hey Brother
text:
When I was younger my brother infuriated me more than anything. It was because of two simple facts. One, that we were kids. Siblings fight; it’s a fact of life. The second is more complex. We are mentally opposites. I take after my mother, living based on emotions and feelings. I let intuition lead me. Kevin takes after our father: logical, analytical, and confident.

So when we grew up all I wanted was his approval. I don’t know what he wanted from me. I assume it was for me to leave him alone. That’s not the case though. He wanted me around to help him with his experiments and adventures. I think I just overwhelmed him. It would frustrate him, then we would fight.

I remember people telling me that I would love my brother when we were older. I could not fathom it. As with most ideas you have as a teenager, it was wrong. I don’t know when it clicked in, but slowly Kevin and I started working. I think we both realized that when our skills were put together, we could accomplish great things.

The countless hours of driving between Breckenridge and Fort Worth is where most of our relationship was built. It was time for just talking, and, at the time, listening to nonstop Dave Matthews Band. It was on our trips to Fort Worth that we forged even closer friendships with Alonzo, Colton, Devin, and Seth.

The thing about Kevin is that he is mostly fearless. It’s a trait that a lot of people think they have but don’t possess. I know he has fears; he just has the ability to ignore them when necessary. It makes it so that he can accomplish anything he sets his mind on.

So when he was eighteen he wanted a house. So he set out, found a house, and bought it. With help from his friends and family, he turned it into a lovely home. I think that the house was for freedom and somewhere to have a wife. It was in that house that Kevin truly found himself. Or at least found his set of goals.

His focus shifted from women to service. He started pioneering and putting his talents towards that. He is a restless person though. So after a few years his thirst for adventure took hold. He put his house on the market and sold it. He then planned a trip across the country.

From Texas to California to Oregon, back down to Oklahoma to join us for Beavers Bend, then back home. A solo trip that would take weeks. My only regret is that I didn’t quit my job, drop everything, and join him. When he got back home, he moved to Fort Worth with Seth. Everything went well, but work was sparse.

This was during my year of not working, so I was sleeping on his couch and eating his leftovers. He was supportive of me during my lost year but always goaded me into being more of an adult. I never can tell how he felt during his time in Fort Worth; I know he enjoyed it. I just never think he felt like it’s where he should have been.

So when money got low and roommates were all getting married, he set another plan. To move to Arizona and live with our great grandfather and see how things went there. It was a change, a shake up. The overall feeling I had was abandonment. I did not hide this feeling. My brother was moving far, far away. He took care of me. I was losing my safety net.

It was all for the best though. He found a new place to grow; I learned how to be more self sufficient. After he was there for a few months, he got invited to BSSB. That led to us going to California for a week that I will never forget. Later the next year we all went to Bethel to visit friends. Kevin has been back to Texas many times. To bring me a car, visit friends, and for funerals.

Even with being away, he has never missed anything. He finds ways to be everywhere he needs to be. We had just gotten back from visiting him a few weeks ago. It’s a trip I could write a lot of blogs about and will. Just this week he was invited to Bethel to serve for a month.

There is still not a person on earth that can frustrate me more. Not a person that can cut to the root of my issues. That calls me out on my flaws without hesitation. Thinks that I need to be a better person. That’s the thing about Kevin: he wants you to be the best person you can be. He sees your potential. When you look at him you see a whimsical mess that is achieving everything he sets his mind to. He deeply cares for his family and friends, even if at times it’s hard to see.

I look forward to whatever is in store for Kevin next. I am blessed to be his brother. I will get to come along on his many adventures. I get someone watching out for me and helping me grow. We don’t often say this to each other, but I love my brother. I don’t know who I would be without him.

title: Hostels
text:
The drive was about an hour long. Alonzo, Asa, and Rachel were in the rental car. Kevin and I in his car. He drives an old Honda shuttle, a car that by all merits should be dead but just keeps going. We were driving from Fairfield to San Francisco. Kevin had just graduated BSSB and we all took a few extra days to explore San Francisco with him.

We had all been planning this to be as cheap as possible. So we chose to stay in a hostel. Something only Kevin and Alonzo had done before. So I wasn’t sure what to expect when we pulled up. I think it was an old army base. When we checked in there was some issue with the rooming. They like to keep the genders separated. We didn’t want Rachel off on her own without Asa, so we all got put in a big room together.

The room just had single bunk beds lining the walls with storage lockers at the bottom for your stuff. I loved this place. It had a big family feeling. Everyone that was staying there was on a big adventure. There was a big common room, a huge kitchen, and a place for everyone to watch movies. Sadly it was Halloween that night, so the festivities were in full swing.

My most vivid memory of the hostel was sitting in the common room late into the night talking and watching videos on our iPads. Every night Alonzo would come and sit with us and promptly fall asleep on the couch. Rachel found a video of Kevin making a funny sound from a day back in Texas. It was the funniest thing in that moment. We played it over and over.

Overall the experience was a great one. I await the days when we won’t have to fear random crazy people. When that sense of family will be felt everywhere. When everyone can go and have adventures, stay wherever, and meet people.

Everyone has a story to tell or dreams to live.

title: King Crab
text:
It kind of felt like being trapped in a small box. No matter what you do the heavy pressure is still there. It was a panic attack. Not my first one, but my most memorable one. I was at a convention a few years back. Without going into many details, I was feeling out of place and abandoned. My anxiety over the situation was rising the whole morning. I don’t know exactly what caused it to finally become too much, but all at once, it was. During a panic attack I run. I go as far away from the source of the pressure.

First I knew I had to find Kevin and tell him that I was bolting. I think I wandered the halls of the convention center four or five times. I found him and said “I have to go man, I have to get out of here.” In turn he told me “Running is never a good idea.” It didn’t matter; I was set and ready to go. I cleared the steps to the doors and pushed them open. Only to be hit by a wall of heat and humidity.

It was June in Texas. Normally that just means unstoppable heat. Just as soon as I got a block away it happened. The sky opened and rained on me. It was fitting. I was crying anyway; why shouldn’t the sky join me? I quickly took off my badge so people wouldn’t see me as a witness leaving the convention early crying. I made it five or six blocks. The rain had slowed and now it was just muggy. I was sweating from the heat. I must have looked a terrible mess. Wet in a suit, slightly crying, looking for somewhere to be.

I made it to the Barnes and Noble. Shuffled in to the bathroom and toweled off. Looking slightly less insane, I went to find a seat. I found one next to a homeless person who was asleep. I just wanted to sit, to go over my situation in my head. It only led to more feelings of uneasiness. I knew that Asa was out and about though. I gave him a call. He seemed confused that I was at the book store and not the convention. Nothing ever startles him though.

He simply said that he would come pick me up soon. That we would go do something. After about thirty minutes, he called and had me come outside. The rescue jeep was sitting there. I crawled in and we were off. He didn’t press me about what happened. He just came and got me. “You want some crab?” he said. I have never turned down crab. So that is simply what we did. It was exactly what was needed.

It just so happened that the soccer world cup was on that afternoon too. So for hours we sat and watched the teams play. Later that day I believe I ended up sitting in a tub crying more. So no, the day did not get better. This day has become something to me. Something other than the panic attack. It is a bright and shining reminder that friends matter. That relationships are vital to my sanity. That I have so many people that will be there when I need them. It makes me question myself. Am I doing the best I can for the people around me? I don’t think I am. I need to be more selfless. More willing to give of myself and my time. These are things I will work on.

title: Lake Day
text:
It was a summer day in Texas. A few clouds lazily floated across the giant blue sky. The heat was immense — Texas summers never did mess around. We loaded up the car and slowly drove to the west. Songs played that reminded us of our youth. A sense of unsureness hung in the air, uncertain that the day would be what we all needed it to become. The dogs panted and waited restlessly in the car. They looked excitedly at all the sights and smells they had never experienced. We pulled into a beer barn and ordered a twelve pack and some ice for our cooler. Everything was set. The sun kept rising, making the day more unbearably hot and dry. That was what we wanted. We were going to the

Lake

.

This is one of my fondest memories. One of the days that you can replay in your mind with great adoration. I don’t want this blog post to be a lamentation of “I wish this was every day” or “I miss this.” That’s not the point. If it were every day it would lose the importance of the moment and become rote. If I missed it, then I would be a fool lingering for things that are long in the past. It was a day where everything lined up. Everything worked. I might be over embellishing it; my memories are 80% fiction. I don’t think that matters. The present is never here. Everything is just a manifestation of your mind. This is my recollection of a day I loved.

The drive to the public swimming area of our lake is only fifteen minutes long. You hit a dirt road and drive past the dam. The dam is where we take people when they first come to Breckenridge. It’s a huge dirt wall. Climbing up its almost vertical face is quite an undertaking. The challenge rewards you with a nice view of the lake at the top. We drove past that to the “beach.” That word is a stretch considering it’s just broken glass and rocks. When we got there it was empty. Asa let the dogs out of the car and they began smelling everything they could. We set up a little area for the boom box we brought. I cracked open a beer and tested out the water. It was cold but not unbearable. With the day only heating up, we knew it would just get better and better. Asa, Rachel, and I slowly waded further into the murky, cold water. At this point Ranger was about a year old and Crosby was still a little puppy. Ranger would swim if you made her, but she really loves chasing at the tide and catching sticks you toss. Crosby just slapped at the water with his paws and whined if you went too far away from him.

We listened to the music, felt the sun feed our cells, and enjoyed watching the playfulness of two puppies experiencing new things. Hours flew by and cheerful conversations were had. Ranger and Crosby kept trying to swim and play on the water. I have trouble expressing my feelings about Asa and Rachel. When I am with them I feel alone. Nothing I do is embarrassing. I never have to second guess anything around them. We are just longtime friends who love each other. After a few hours the dogs were worn out; we packed up and took them home. After much needed food, we fetched some floating devices so that true floating relaxation could be had. Back to the lake we went, sans the dogs. During the afternoon an old lady brought her iguana and let it swim around for a while. That was the only contact we had with the outside world that day: one bizarre encounter. Apart from that, the lake was ours. It was odd: the many times I had been here, people came and went constantly. This, however, was our day.

After swimming we explored the dam and went home. That’s all. That’s all the day was: nothing extreme or extravagant. It was simply true friends relaxing on a day where no one bothered us. We were allowed to float and talk and exist with nothing to weigh us down. Everything worked. The only plan at the outset of the day was to take the dogs to the lake for a bit. It took all of 10 seconds to plan. I often feel like things get over planned and forced to happen. On this day we will do this and it will be so. From Fancy Day I know that sometimes you must plan things out like that. I like to keep in mind, though, that simple days without the burden of thought can be just as fulfilling. You just need a place to be and people that you want to be with. No frills — just people and time.

title: Musa Moses
text:
He moved here to become a pilot and learn more Mandarin. I’m not sure what got him to come to his first Swahili meeting. Being from Kenya he was taught both Swahili and English along with his smaller tribal language, Kikuyu. He started to come to the Monday night meetings early like I did. Quiet and shy is his nature, so it was good that it was just me and him. It gave me time to break through that shell. Over the first few weeks I learned about some of his life, and he taught me a few Swahili words. He also expressed to me how scared he was of going on the stage. Little did he know what the next year and few months would hold for him.

We started trying to have Swahili nights. Asa, Rachel, Musa, and myself. They never went far, but it was valuable time for us to get to know each other. We learned about his eating habits. Whole cooked chickens and broccoli were all it sounded like he ate. I remember the first time we made fajitas at Asa’s house. Super simple: cooked chicken and sautéed peppers and onions. I guess living in Texas and being around Mexican food has made me forget that others haven’t experienced it. So I had to show him how to make his first fajita. I think he liked it; I don’t think he liked the tamales he had a few weeks later though.

It was at one of these Swahili nights that the topic of driving to Arizona to see Kevin came up. It looked like it would be just Seth, Asa, and I. So we invited Musa. It was with little hesitation that he agreed to go. We ironed out more details and got our days together. “I’m kinda confused about Moses coming…” said Kevin the next time we Skyped. I assured him it would be cool. On that trip we got closer to Musa and found out how funny he can be when you are listening to what he is saying. He taught us about Project Friday and how any day can be a project if you want it to be. We hiked in barren places and swam in flowing rivers. He ‘lost’ his glasses at a disc golf course and proceeded to run the whole course looking for them. Hint: they were at Kevin’s house. We spent nights all talking to him about American girls and his future plans.

Musa is a hard worker. I don’t know any other way to put it. Even with all his anxieties he will never say no to an assignment. He became a vital part of our congregation when he took over translating duties for English parts. There is no better way to get over a fear of the stage than by being on it every single meeting. He has now given two full public talks; from what I understood of them, they went great. For our one day special assembly he translated a few of the parts. The whole time pioneering and keeping a great, happy, willing spirit. He is currently helping out with the Dallas Swahili group as well. He is boundless.

So that’s why I am sad to see him go home. Over the last year he has become a close friend and an excellent brother. He has inspired me to keep facing my fears when they arise. He has helped me with every single part I have ever given in Swahili, so warning: everyone, they might start getting worse. I couldn’t imagine what it was like for him to move across the world to a place like Texas. I hope that it has become a second home for him though. That his time here was well spent. I will miss you, Musa River Basket Mwangi. I know that this isn’t the end of my story with him though — it’s only the beginning.

title: Naked man
text:
It was right at the peak of dejection. We had just crossed back over the river to the shore we hiked to. Seth, Kevin, and Asa took the path up farther to see if there was another way. Moses and I were sitting on the rocks letting our clothes dry out in the sun and wringing out our socks. The heat from the sun was welcome warmth after the ice cold river water. It was then that it happened: the naked man ran past.

He was in his 50s or 60s with peppery grey hair and a long glorious beard. I saw him off in the distance running towards us. I chose not to tell Moses early. I wanted to see his reaction at this sight. I turned and checked the path the other boys went down to see Kevin and Asa slowly coming back.

He was running at a faster pace than I thought he was, and when I looked again he was on us. Moses gave a slight noise of surprise and the old man just said “Hello” as he ran past. The only thing I was worried about was his feet. How can you run on such a rocky surface with no shoes? So as his old tanned skin zoomed past I looked at his feet. He had simple sandals on. It looked like he had made them himself.

He was at that moment a few seconds from Kevin and Asa. I watched as he ran at them. Neither of the boys had any look of reaction on their faces. Kevin took him for what he was, a local. He took the time to ask him about other paths to take and advice on what to do. Or at least I assume that’s what they were talking about. After they were done he waved and kept on his run.

This moment vividly sticks out in my mind as important. Well, that might not be the right word. It was enlightening. You must know that we were out in the middle of a barren land. Off a road that is painful to drive on. Yet here was a mountain man, a naked mountain man. He was running in the early afternoon stark naked. Not a care in the world. At that moment he was possessionless.

Yet on that same hike I had brought a fifty pound pack and was about to die from exhaustion. He was everything I wasn’t. Everything that in my mind I would like to be. I know I can’t run away from my responsibilities. But the idea of a simple life is something I struggle with. It might not seem like that is the case. I have few possessions and lack the means to gather lots of things. I do have the want though.

It’s in the back of my head to distract myself with people and things. To keep my life busy and full so I don’t let my creeping issues catch up with me. The naked man though, he had nothing in that moment. He had no music, no phone, and no clothes. So what is simple to me? If I leave my phone at home do I get panicky? Why is it that I try to keep myself distracted at all times? Is that our generation? I want to start taking some time to be nothing and have nothing.

title: On the floor
text:
I will be the first one to tell you that I am a hopeless romantic. I fall in love with at least two women a day. In a second I can see my whole life with them flash by. That’s all though; they keep walking by and I keep my gaze on the horizon. I read books, sing to songs, and watch movies that blaze this idea of love and romance on my mind. Yet single I am. I often ask myself if it’s a choice I make. I want it to be. I want to say I could be settled out with a few kids if I wasn’t trying to be where I am with the choices I’ve made.

Don’t get me wrong, finding a mate is a great thing. I would be overjoyed if I had found one by now. I just have yet to find one that jumps out at me. So this isn’t a dating or marriage bashing post. It’s simply what has been on my mind. Because this singleness isn’t easy mentally. You can tell yourself over and over “I’m not ready. I’m not ready.” It just takes one girl to upend that.

I sleep on the floor. The floor. In a room that I rent for next to nothing. I barely can pay my bills. I have wildly out of control depression. I truly am not ready. My imagination gets in the way of that though. I can see all the good things. All the happy moments. It’s completely skewed towards the overly positive ideas of relationships. I wonder if everyone does this? But are they like me, that they talk sternly to their mind afterwards?

Here is the kicker though. After I write this on the floor, I’m going to eat some cookies and watch whatever I want. I’m going to keep the fan on high. I’m going to fart as much as I please. I will talk out loud to myself as I do. I am free. If I wanted to drive to Montana tomorrow, I could. That is something that I do enjoy. But I worry that this so-called freedom is just an excuse that we come up with to make ourselves not so bummed out.

In summary, I don’t know. I feel like I love where I’m at. Then I see a girl that I’ve loved, or think I could, and that whole perception comes crashing down. At twenty-four I thought I would have this more figured out. No one has anything figured out. We are all just blindly shooting darts at a wall hoping something sticks. So this is to you, Tom: your happiness is only dependent on your mindset. You can be lonely whether you are single or not.

I think I might drive to Montana now.

title: Open to Opportunities
text:
My mind is my most formidable enemy. It tells me to stay home and to do little. It tells me that I need rest. It tells me to not make plans. For the longest time I thought I was an overly extroverted person. I thought I survived on being out with people. It has taken time, but I now see that I’m entirely introverted. I can’t survive without people and the relationships I have, but I also need lots of time alone. Many times I have simply stayed home. It has made me miss out on things that I desperately wish I hadn’t. With that knowledge I tend to not listen to my mind. I try to know myself and trick my brain into letting me do things.

It was Monday — I have every Monday off. On a normal Monday there is laundry to do, rooms to clean, and studying to happen. This weekend, though, I had missed out on seeing Seth. Since the move to Fort Worth we have tried to not let a week go by without seeing each other. A week had almost gone by. I texted him to see what he was doing. “I’m home sick,” he said. “With what?” I replied. If it was explosive diarrhea I would have stayed home. “Congestion, and general death,” he said.

I have little fear of the flu or the cold since my time at CVS. I’m not saying that I can’t catch them. It’s just that after days of people coughing on you and not getting sick, it makes you confident. Seth being sick didn’t scare me. It was the fact that it was 11:30AM and at 6PM I needed to be home to get prepared for meeting. In retrospect, that seems like a huge amount of time, but then it seemed like too little time to warrant a visit. My broken mind will think of all the time spent traveling and gas wasted and food eaten and money spent. It will look at every negative thing to keep me from moving.

My best defense is to just not think. To just act. That’s what happened. Before I knew it I was in my car, sans socks, driving down I-20 to Seth’s house. I was there in the blink of three songs. At the door I found Phoebe barking at me. Being protective of her sick owner. After some reassuring words from her owner and the sniffing of my hand, she remembered who I was. Then for what seemed like a blink, Seth and I recounted our last week. Filled each other in on what was going on. Talked of future plans. That blink was two hours.

That’s how he and I have always been. For the longest time we have been bonded by an invisible link. We understand each other fully. I believe that if stranded on an island with one person of my choosing, I would take him. The conversations would never tire and time would pass quickly. I don’t like using the term best friend. I have many friends and I think of most of them as best. This is the blessing of my life. I have many “brothers” that I consider the closest of people. Seth is one of the first to join this group in my mind. Some have left it and some have joined; I have no doubt that he will be a permanent member.

During our talking, his sister Rachel texted him to see if he needed anything. We needed someone to drive us to get food. So we summoned her to us. Rachel could be defined as a loving person. Constantly caring and trying to find the best of things for others. So, when she showed up to Seth’s place, convincing her to take us out to eat wasn’t hard. She agreed immediately. It might have helped that the place we suggested had free food for the day.

The rest of the day I won’t go into much detail. I want it to purely remain mine for the time. I will say that we sat for over four hours talking and laughing. It was a true gem and something that with them has happened many times, yet still delights me to the core. What is the point of all this? What if I had let my mind think? What if I had let it be the negative homebody that it wants to be? Then I would have stayed home. Nothing productive would have happened. Isolation never leads to anything of lasting value. Instead I was uplifted. I was allowed to be with those whom I love. Don’t waste opportunities. Reject negative thoughts. Be open to opportunities and never worry about being tired. The time for resting can be found later.

title: Party Adam
text:
I have different modes for different situations. One of my closest friends called one of them ‘Party Adam’. It was a mask I put on when I was in an area with more than six people. Basically I would up my energy level and become a social butterfly. All my conversations were vapid and lasted about four minutes, then I would frantically leave and talk to someone else. The whole time never making a lot of eye contact and continually looking around finding exits. She hated ‘Party Adam’ and once she pointed it out I grew to hate him too. None of the interactions I was having had any merit or value. I guess you would call it small talk, but I knew how to make it seem like I was interested. Then by jumping around to so many people it caused me to think I was popular.

Once she pointed out ‘Party Adam’ you can directly draw a line to where I started to withdraw and avoid parties and gatherings. I was trying to deepen my relationships and avoid uselessness, but that only caused me to avoid my issues. It took a few years for me to understand that ‘Party Adam’ was a coping mechanism. I had severe anxiety and thought it was completely normal to worry and panic all the time. Most of my different modes were coping mechanisms. Avoiding parties or gatherings was not helping my situation but rather allowing me to not grow as a person. I have come to a better understanding of who I am and the difficulty my brain has. I have been able to get rid of these different versions of myself. I am currently more myself than I have ever been.

I wonder if my close friends have noticed. I assume not, because generally they got me, the real me. Now I will try to focus in on a few people at a party or a big gathering. I try to dig deeply into their life and the things that interest them. What experiences they have had and what knowledge they can share with me. I am aware that I am at my max with friends. I just can’t handle many more people than I currently care deeply about. Rather than let that be a reason for not getting to know the deeper side of someone, or be vapid, I use it as a way to learn. If I can just absorb a bit of everyone’s experience it might just make me a more well rounded person. If I’m just now understanding a bit of what makes me, me. If this is twenty five, then what will thirty be? Sixty?

Will forever be long enough to understand who you are? What makes you behave the way you are? Now is the time for me to practice not worrying. Not thinking about time or situations. To be present in my life. I don’t think it’s fully possible, but I’m willing to try. This year is going to be about being present. Not to live in my worried mind.

title: Past Positions
text:
I am now settled. I feel calm again, not filled with unknowing and anxiety. A few months ago my life was in limbo. One of my longtime friends and housemate at the time was getting married and moving on. The landlord of the house we stayed at was preparing the house to sell it. My job was in a different town and it was an hour and twenty minute drive every day for me. Money was tight and moving anywhere else seemed impossible without a roommate and a lot more money. My journey to Fort Worth and Swahili was not one I was ready to part with. As I have stated before, I have issues with getting overwhelmed. When overwhelmed I freeze up.

This often makes my issues much worse. I will either wait till the last moment to fix the issue or I will just let it pass and fail to act. This is one of my flaws. So it was my goal not to freeze up, and for the most part I succeeded. I did stop writing though. I stopped most of the creative projects that keep my mind active. So here is what you missed. Most of my mental activities focused on Alonzo. He was starting a new life, so I wanted to enjoy the most of the time I had left with him.

Getting married changes things. You can tell me over and over that it won’t or didn’t — you are wrong. Putting two people’s lives together is hard and not something to be toyed with. So I was happy with Lonzo’s choice to move to my home town Breckenridge. It was a small area where they could spend time truly becoming one. Without the distractions that the city has to offer. Even with the idea of spending as much time with Lonzo as I could, I still had little time. Work to go to, meetings and service to make, and weddings to prepare for. At the same time I was looking for places to live. A process that is shockingly hard.

I love my one on one time with Lonzo. Most of our activities together are spent talking. In the house we lived in that was mostly at night after a week of not seeing each other. Turns would be taken telling of our weeks and the plans for the upcoming weeks. Ideas expressed and advice asked for. That is what true friendship is to me. Two people whose identities can be expressed not by what they do or what activities they share in. But in the conversations they have and the value of said conversations. I’ve had years of delightful conversations with Lonzo. I plan to have many lifetimes of conversations with him.

Things worked out. Lonzo and Demi’s wedding was lovely. I found a place to live and got transferred to a store that is much closer. I do understand why things worked out the way they did. Such a hard concept to grasp: “Do not be anxious over anything…” I wonder how the moon feels about all this. Over the course of human history it has kept its orbit. Once around the earth every twenty-seven days. Every single person who has ever lived has taken a moment to look at the moon, to bask in its gentle glow. Rarely do they look to it as an example. It follows the course set out for it; it does just so. When I look at the moon I think of a lot of things. My goal is to look at it now and see what it is teaching me. That everything will be ok — follow the path set out and stay calm.

title: Real Talk
text:
I keep trying to decide how frank I should be. Am I willing to let my deepest thoughts be shared with the public? My roommate and close friend Seth got married early in 2014, which left me alone in our apartment. The bills were manageable and they would be moving in below me. What I did not expect is how important it was for me to not be alone. When I moved to Fort Worth I had the fortune of moving in with Don and Lonzo. I kept to myself even in that situation but knew they were around, available. Then I moved in with Seth to mostly the same situation. So this was my first time truly alone in my life. In the simplest of terms: left on my own, I give up.

I call it depression, but that’s only a small factor in it. It’s the combination of self loathing, anxiety, depression, and laziness that do it. For the first few months I could control it. I went to meetings and work, only missing a few. As time went on it was like weight pushing on my shoulders. It was the small voice that says ‘no one cares. no one will miss you. why get up’ that grows every day. It was the overthinking about everything that caused the joy to be siphoned from my life. For example, going to meeting was an 18 minute drive. That’s all it took to cause me to miss countless meetings. That small drive made me feel like puking every Monday and Saturday.

For those of you that are reading this that struggle with similar circumstances, I feel for you. The remnant that don’t understand, I envy you. Take some time to try to think about what anxiety or depression is like. Know that the best thing you can do for a person with these issues is just to be around and quiet. The most calming thing to me is just knowing that someone is around. One of my dearest friends is Wes; he often is what I need, but during one of my most recent panic attacks he said something strange. He found me on the floor between a bed and a wall sobbing my eyes out, with my cousin Seth calmly and quietly sitting in the room with me. He kept telling me to get up, get up. After I ignored his requests he touched my arm and told me, “I will leave, but I need you to tell me you will be ok first.”

My mind spirals. Ok? At that moment death was preferable to anything, as awful as that is to say. Would I be ok? I couldn’t even fathom being ok ever again for the rest of my life. What was ok? Was I going to just get up and a light would turn on and I would smile? Ok was the farthest thing from what I could be. I firmly said, “Seth.” It’s a sad bond we share, understanding each other’s panic attacks and dark thoughts. He got Wes to leave the room. Now, in my calm normal state, I can see how much concern and love Wes was showing me. He is truly a loving friend who I would do anything for. Sometimes though the best course is to just be with the person.

So in the last two months of living on my own I rarely left my home other than to buy terrible food and binge eat. I would call in to work a bunch, and when I was there I did little to no work. I stopped going to meetings, thinking I was the worst person alive and shouldn’t show my face. The idea of killing myself became more and more appealing. Thankfully I could recognize my situations at times and see that I needed out. I am a 25 year old man and I needed help. I was drowning. I still have loving parents who did not even hesitate when I asked to move home, and I will forever be even more in debt to them for that.

So here I am. Home. Am I ok? I don’t know, but so far I feel as light as a feather.

Hoping to keep writing. Check back for more.

title: Tons of Texts
text:
I often find myself “bored” at work. I live in a constant attention generation, a check-your-phone-every-ten-minutes type of life. I wonder what it was like before phones. What an eight hour shift would be like every single day with no outside stimulation. Of course the work they were doing 30 years ago might not be quite as dull as what I do. I do spend a lot of time at work without my phone. I take time to doodle on notepads, try to make things out of trash, and learn new skills. Right now I’m working on learning some drumming patterns. A large part of work is learning new Swahili words, but I use my phone for that.

With my phone I can do things like that. Find new words to learn, look at friends’ photos, google things I want to learn, and text people. When I grew up we had instant messaging services. They were all people did. I think of the time spent on the computer just chatting away late into the night. How did I have so many things to say? IMing has just turned into texting now though. Still I wonder how I have so many things to say.

Some days though I need something to ponder, something to keep my mind working on. I try to think of something thought provoking, a question. Then I spam it out to everyone that I think will reply. I always have a competition in my mind to see who has the best reply. Almost every time it is Seth; he knows my sense of humor though. This last question I sent was a simple one. “How would you describe yourself in one word? How would you describe me in one word?”

It’s a two part question for a simple reason. No one wants to describe themselves, and it makes my thirst for viewpoints on me seem less vain. The replies were as expected, generally self deprecating towards themselves and overselling me. I have humble, sweet friends though, so it’s alright. I will say a few I got about me that I had to ponder over: esoteric, beautiful, inquisitive, zigzag, gentle, and renaissance.

Once again I think these words oversell me quite a bit. I can see threads of the meaning in my life though, so I can understand it. Zigzag confused me; I had to ask Alonzo what it meant. This is what he said: “You go where you go, tough to follow but fun to try. Always changing, progressing but pulled in different directions.” It’s nice to have friends that know you well. That understand your oddities.

So one day if you get a text from me with an odd question, ponder it. Come up with a real response and know that I will be thinking over what you say for the rest of the day. I’m glad I have a phone at work — the ability to deepen my useless knowledge of things that will only matter to me and my incessant non-resting mind.

title: Work & Ideas
text:
I feel the tinge of embarrassment when people ask me what I do for work. I work as a cashier at a large chain pharmacy. The first thing people say is “so you’re a pharmacy tech?”. No, no I’m not. I sell candy, laundry detergent, and toilet paper. I get yelled at on a regular basis for things that are out of my control. Often people think my job is nothing more than pressing buttons and handing out money. I like to view it as much more: a way to affect people in a small manner. I make the work that I do enjoyable.

My parents taught me that work was work. Never to shy away or be embarrassed at what you do. My mother has proudly cleaned toilets for the majority of her life. She still does that from time to time, but mainly she manages the household of an older gentleman whom she has lovingly watched over for twenty years. My father was an office manager and is now an IT supervisor. Nothing was out of reach for them, but they knew that the job doesn’t make the person. So when I was little they had me do jobs. Mostly at my mother’s cleaning jobs. Emptying the little trash buckets at about twenty desks. I think I made like five or ten dollars a week doing that.

It taught me the value of work. That when you did a job and did it well you were rewarded. I remember going to the gas station and buying my first Red Bull. It was something that my mother would never have bought for me and still won’t. I was able to feel the excitement of buying it without asking anyone if I could or should. I just acted. The can was cold in my hands, the subtle pop of cracking the top. The odd and bitter taste is still vivid in my mind. It was the first thing I remember buying on my own.

At around fifteen I got a job at our local grocery store. I worked four days a week from eight to one, twenty hours a week. I was doing full time ministry work at the time and had just started homeschooling. It was there I met and learned from people like Ann and Gary. Both in their fifties but still working every day. Longer and harder than I could have. I was soft. The first day home I laid in bed and tried to calm my spasming muscles. They had never been exerted so much. It took time of me feeling incompetent and lost. Soon I got into the rhythm and learned that my job was less important than how I could affect people. Make people feel more or less comfortable. Implant small thoughts and ideas in their minds that would blossom in time.

For example, Neil deGrasse Tyson once tweeted about what the color of stop lights would be if our blood wasn’t red. I found amusement in thinking about this idea. So the next day at work I would pose the question to customers to see what they thought. A month had gone by and a woman comes in and said that she had been thinking about it ever since that day and that it affected how she viewed everything. It was an idea spreading from Neil to me to her. Rather than just taking her money and whisking her away, I was able to change the way she thought in a small manner. Or the older people that I take time to dote on. We don’t normally take things out to people’s cars, but with my older returning customers I take time for them. I like to show them that not all of my generation is cold and in a rush. That we can slowly walk with them and talk about the past that they lived.

So over the years and different jobs I’ve had, the goal has been the same. See how you can affect people, change and shape their views. Do it in a quick and subtle manner. Asking odd or abrasive questions, not following the unspoken social rules we have. Do unexpected loving things for people. When I go in to a store I feel so sad for the person checking me out. They just look despondent and uncaring of me and what my life is. They don’t seem to notice my narrative because they are so stuck in their own. So when people come to my line I want to be different. I want to be a brother or a son, I want to be a friend. I don’t want anyone to ever look at me and feel bad for me. I don’t feel bad for what I do.

title: Bread
text:
When your hands are covered in flour you have only one thing you can do. Knead dough and shape dough. I don't know when I made my first loaf of bread. I’m sure that it was heavy and awful; baking is in my blood though. I have flashbacks to my granny's shop and all the wonderful things she made. I grew up there eating lemon bars, brownies, cheesecake, apple pies, and the cake tops that she lovingly frosted and layered to look like small cakes for us. Pulling handfuls of chocolate chips out of the big Tupperware container was one of my favorite things to do. I have a major sweet tooth.

My recent interest in bread has been purely medical. When my brain gets overactive, underactive, or any other way, I need an escape. Something mindless and repetitive. Something that yielded a result that was tangible. When I am anxious or depressed I think in scenarios and figments. Nothing is real and everything is fading. So to be able to make something that you can then touch, cut, eat, and share positively is satisfying and calming.

Bread is three things. Flour. Yeast. Water. Other than that you can add whatever you want, but at the core it is those three things. Feel like adding an egg? Olive oil? Honey? Do it. Who cares. This is your bread. How will you know till you have tried? I have never measured. I rarely think about what I’m doing. I just put warm water in a bowl. Yeast into that warm water. Stir. Wait. Add flour. Stir. Wait. If the yeast has activated the mixture will be soupy and bubbly. Add more flour till a slightly sticky dough has formed. Wait for it to rise. Then my favorite part. Kneading. Laying this sticky mess on a floured counter and kneading it till it becomes a smooth, not sticky, elastic ball.

Then you wait again. That might be the best part. Waiting. It’s the time to sit in a chair and have a reason to just sit there. Someone asks what you are doing? Tell them, “I’m waiting for my dough to rise.” Just wait for forty minutes to pass. Work some more. Wait. Repeat. I think it’s like breathing exercises for me. Taking time to just calm down and remember that everything is going to be alright. Sadly this leads to my least favorite part of the whole process. Baking your bread. I over check my bread. I do it every time. I just hate burning things so bad.

Generally it turns out good. Then after spending a few hours making it, you don't want to eat it. Give it a few hours though and I will eat the whole thing. That’s why it is best to give it away as fast as I can. If not I’m going to get so fat. Giving it away is also great because it makes you feel useful. Something you just made is going to help someone to live another day. Most times they are happy to get bread too. Who dislikes bread?

That is my mental therapy. It brings me back to my childhood. It makes me feel connected to my baking family history. It is calming repetition. It is a creative outlet. It makes your house smell amazing. In the end it is bread, warm soft bread.

What is your creative therapy? Let me know, I might want to try it.

-Tom

title: The Spot
text:
Right after the dam it’s a quick left. Down a slope, over a speed bump, and around a giant pothole. It is the bottom of the dam; a fast moving river runs next to you. It looks to be a useless, unassuming place. Twenty steps away is a small babbling brook. Not so small that you can hop over it, but not so big that it’s a giant worry. It’s the first hurdle, a small one but an important one. It’s this brook that caused me to almost not go on this hike in the first place. I’m not a sure footed person, a bit top heavy as some would say. Yet as most things go I was encouraged to go forward years ago.
